Notes: Total: 394,748 MT CO2e (2018). Unincorporated county pop ~154,000 (2018 est). Down 27% from 2005 baseline of 539,333 MT CO2e. Transportation 50.8%; Residential 26.3%; Commercial/Industrial 13.9%; Solid Waste 8.3%; Wastewater 0.7%. Also: 2019 county-wide (all jurisdictions) total was 691,262 MT CO2e per AMBAG.
Source: County of Santa Cruz Draft 2018 Community-Wide GHG Inventory Report (AMBAG, Feb 2021)

Notes: Meets US EPA standard (12 ug/m3) and WHO 2005 guideline (10 ug/m3). Exceeds WHO 2021 revised guideline (5 ug/m3). 2017: 7.5; 2018: 8.5 (wildfire-influenced). Avg 5.2 unhealthy PM2.5 days/year (2016-2018).
Source: DataShare SCC; American Lung Association State of the Air

Notes: Parks, reserves and other protected open space cover 32% of the RCIS area. County is a global biodiversity hotspot: 1,000+ native plant species, 17 endemic to county, 35 endemic species total. Habitats include old-growth redwood, Santa Cruz sandhills, karst caves, coastal prairie, maritime chaparral.
Source: Santa Cruz County Regional Conservation Investment Strategy (RCIS), approved by CDFW April 2023

Indicator: Nitrogen leached to groundwater per year
Data Point: 87% of nitrate from agricultural sources in Pajaro Valley
Notes: Pajaro Valley Groundwater Basin has significantly elevated nitrate from fertilizer. Sources: 87% agricultural, 5% stream runoff, 4% sewer leakage, 4% septic. Pajaro Valley Water Management Agency managing seawater intrusion and nutrient loading. North county watersheds (San Lorenzo) have lower agricultural intensity.
Source: Pajaro Valley Water Management Agency; PVWMA Salt and Nutrient Management Plan

Notes: County-level data from CA DPR. 67% of applied pesticides are fumigant gases — highest proportion of any CA county. Concentrated in Pajaro Valley agriculture (strawberries, lettuce). Key fumigants include chloropicrin and 1,3-dichloropropene.
Source: CA Department of Pesticide Regulation, 2022 Pesticide Use Report for Santa Cruz County

Indicator: Percent of waste sent to landfill
Notes: County achieved 75% diversion rate target set in 2005. County has Zero Waste long-term goal. SB 1383 (2022) requires organics collection. Buena Vista Drive Sanitary Landfill is primary county facility. Solid waste accounted for 8.3% of county GHG emissions (32,700 MT CO2e in 2018).
Source: Santa Cruz County Public Works; CalRecycle Disposal Reporting System
